channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: U.S., Iran Prepare for Talks on Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Tehran insists that it has sole authority over the waterway. Washington isn’t convinced.

item.title: Turkey’s economic plan to win from the Iran war
item.description: The country is a case study in the spillovers of conflict—both positive and negative

item.title: The Supreme Court Pushes Back on Trump’s Fed Assault
item.description: Tampering with the Federal Reserve’s independence proved to be a bridge too far.

item.title: Should every baby’s DNA be sequenced?
item.description: The genomic generation is on its way

item.title: America’s Balkan policy is all about gas
item.description: It is using its terminal in Krk to shoulder the green EU aside

item.title: Farmers Worldwide Brace for El Niño
item.description: An intense El Niño threatens to compound trade and energy pain.

item.title: How Americans see their country’s past, present and future
item.description: As America turns 250, our Economist/YouGov poll reveals a proud but deeply divided nation

item.title: What If Russia Asks Kazakhstan for Fuel?
item.description: Kazakhstan could plug Russia's current gasoline deficit, but it's a risk. Astana struggles already with self-sufficiency.

item.title: Hong Kong, once a great place to raise and spend money, is halfway back
item.description: Tighter ties with the mainland have not always helped

item.title: Can NATO Pull Off a Dull Summit?
item.description: European countries are hoping that Erdogan can keep Trump from causing chaos.

item.title: The AI boom and geopolitics are rewiring Asia’s oceans
item.description: New cables between data centres are avoiding China and chokepoints

item.title: The rise of vibe lawyering
item.description: AI is emboldening people to represent themselves in court. The results are mixed

item.title: So far, so fast
item.description: Our report on gay marriage from 2014

item.title: Why Americans Are Wary of AI
item.description: The tech industry is asking Americans to embrace AI at a moment when trust in government is declining. While these may seem like separate issues, persuading people to trust AI will be far more difficult if they do not trust the institutions responsible for regulating it.

item.title: Unfit
item.description: Our leader on Donald Trump’s response to a deadly far-right rally in Charlottesville in 2017

item.title: Months After Attacks, Chinese Work Resumes on the Dushanbe-Kulma Highway
item.description: Chinese workers have reportedly resumed construction on a critical highway along the Afghanistan-Tajikistan border where two such workers were killed in November 2025.

item.title: ‘Subversive Carrots’ and China’s Economic Influence Over the World
item.description: “Economic statecraft is not as simple or easy as Chinese decisionmakers think or as U.S. policymakers and external observers might fear,” argues Audrye Wong, author of a new book on the topic.

item.title: America’s Sexual Exceptionalism
item.description: How Washington shaped global policy toward sex and prostitution.

item.title: Whales, Cars, Farms, and Parks
item.description: How the United States remade the global environment from 1776 to today.

item.title: With America in Retreat, Europe Now Depends on Ukraine
item.description: Europeans are gaming out their future defense, and it’s unthinkable without Kyiv.

item.title: The 2016 South China Sea Arbitration Award at 10: Its Lasting Significance
item.description: The award did not settle maritime disputes in the region. But it did fundamentally clarify the interpretation of maritime entitlements under UNCLOS – and forced countries to clarify their claims in turn.

item.title: To Compete in the Pacific, the US Needs to Move Past Zero-Sum Alignment
item.description: The U.S. wants explicit alignment, but China’s more flexible approach to strategic competition resonates with Pacific Island states.

item.title: Why the Gulf Needs Economic Ties with Iran
item.description: Most Gulf observers agree that the old security order is dying. But none of the most commonly proposed options—buying more advanced weapons, seeking protection from new partners in the Global South, or building stronger national militaries—is sufficient on its own.

item.title: China’s New Investment Regulations Block Strategic Technology Transfer
item.description: By adding legal backup to state control of Chinese private companies, Decree No. 837 affects any country receiving their investments. This regulation matters well beyond China itself.

item.title: How Land Swaps Are Reshaping Border Politics Between Uzbekistan and Kyrgyzstan
item.description: A region once associated with security crises, mined frontiers, and closed border crossings is increasingly characterized by negotiation, pragmatic compromise, and regional connectivity.

item.title: Why Central Asia Is Turning to Putin for Nuclear Power
item.description: Will Central Asia’s fix for its energy crisis merely trigger another?

item.title: The Right Way to Tackle Developing Countries’ Cancer Crisis
item.description: Some health advocates believe that weakening intellectual-property protections and capping prices for the most advanced cancer treatments would expand access in developing countries. But this well-intentioned argument overlooks an inescapable reality: without strong incentives for innovation, such treatments would not exist.

item.title: 3 Governments, 1 China Policy? Dhaka’s Evolving Engagement With Beijing
item.description: While Bangladesh's domestic politics have undergone huge change over the past two years, its relations with China have displayed greater continuity than rupture.

item.title: Why the Heart of India’s Indigenous Fighter Ecosystem Will be American for Several Decades to Come
item.description: While negotiations for the licensed manufacture of F414 jet engines in India continue, the off-the-shelf procurement cost of some F414 engines for the AMCA prototype has tripled.

item.title: Europe Will Never Be an AI Superpower
item.description: But it’s not too late for the continent to claim some technological independence for itself.

item.title: Enforce AI Guardrails Before It’s Too Late
item.description: The Trump administration’s decision to impose export controls on Anthropic’s latest models is emblematic of the incoherent and shifting response from policymakers worldwide to the rapidly advancing AI sector. To ensure that these tools do not court disaster, governments must establish meaningful guardrails.

item.title: The Cost of America Abandoning the Military Draft
item.description: Trump could be confident his war in Iran would not touch the daily lives of most voters.

item.title: Europe Goes Its Own Way
item.description: Drifting from America, the continent Is rearming and reordering itself.

item.title: Bangkok’s Voters Hand Chadchart Second Term as Governor By Decisive Margin
item.description: Chadchart Sittipunt, an independent, has replicated the landslide victory that delivered him to the governor's office in 2022.

item.title: Why Pakistan Struggles With Violence Against Women
item.description: There are no official statistics on acid attacks on women in Pakistan. But an estimated 200 cases are reported each year.

item.title: Indonesia Says Tech Firms Have Wiped 4.7M Child Accounts Since Govt Ban
item.description: Jakarta is one of a growing cohort of nations seeking to safeguard under-16s from cyberbullying, online fraud, and pornography.
